text: Both eccentric training and heavy slow resistance training produce significant improvements in Achilles tendinopathy symptoms and function after 12 weeks, with improvements maintained at 52 weeks.
population: patients with chronic midportion Achilles tendinopathy

text: Heavy slow resistance training and eccentric training are equally effective for treating midportion Achilles tendinopathy, with no significant differences in clinical or structural outcomes.
population: patients with chronic midportion Achilles tendinopathy

text: Training compliance is significantly higher with heavy slow resistance training (92%) compared to eccentric training (78%) for Achilles tendinopathy rehabilitation.
population: patients with chronic midportion Achilles tendinopathy

text: Patient satisfaction tends to be higher with heavy slow resistance training (100%) than eccentric training (80%) at 12 weeks, though the difference is not statistically significant.
specific_value: 100% HSR vs 80% ECC satisfaction at 12 weeks (P = .052)
population: patients with chronic midportion Achilles tendinopathy

text: Loading-based treatment for Achilles tendinopathy produces significant reductions in tendon thickness and neovascularization alongside clinical improvement.
population: patients with chronic midportion Achilles tendinopathy

text: Achilles tendinopathy symptoms present for longer than 3 months (chronic) respond well to structured loading programs of 12 weeks duration.
population: patients with chronic midportion Achilles tendinopathy
